disease.name: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
disease.note: Chronic cholestatic liver disease characterized by progressive inflammation, fibrosis, and stricturing of intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. Strongly associated with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), especially ulcerative colitis. High risk of cholangiocarcinoma and colorectal cancer.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Acute cholangitis or dominant stricture -> URGENT ERCP/antibiotics; decompensated cirrhosis -> TRANSPLANT evaluation; asymptomatic -> SURVEILLANCE

examFeatures.note: Ascites, caput medusae, spider angiomas in cirrhosis

laboratory.name: ALP (alkaline phosphatase)
laboratory.indication: elevated in cholestasis; may fluctuate spontaneously; normal ALP does not exclude PSC

laboratory.name: Total and direct bilirubin
laboratory.indication: elevated in dominant stricture or advanced disease; fluctuating

laboratory.name: Serum IgG4
laboratory.indication: elevated in IgG4-SC (mimic); 9% of PSC have mildly elevated IgG4

laboratory.name: CA 19-9
laboratory.indication: elevated in cholangiocarcinoma; >129 U/mL has 79% sensitivity; biliary obstruction itself can elevate; use with caution

laboratory.name: Liver biopsy
laboratory.indication: not required for diagnosis if MRCP diagnostic. Used if small-duct PSC suspected (normal MRCP) or to stage fibrosis. Classic: onion-skin periductal fibrosis.

imaging.name: MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography)
imaging.indication: first-line imaging diagnosis: multifocal strictures and dilations (beaded appearance), intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile duct involvement; assesses for dominant stricture

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: bile duct wall thickening, dilated ducts above strictures, gallstones, cirrhosis, HCC screening; may be normal in early disease

imaging.name: ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography)
imaging.indication: therapeutic: balloon dilation of dominant strictures, stent placement, brushings for cytology if cholangiocarcinoma suspected. Not diagnostic first-line (risk of pancreatitis, cholangitis).

imaging.name: Contrast-enhanced CT abdomen
imaging.indication: assess for cholangiocarcinoma mass, cirrhosis, portal hypertension

imaging.name: Colonoscopy with biopsies
imaging.indication: mandatory at PSC diagnosis to screen for IBD (often asymptomatic); IBD surveillance q1-2 years

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis; EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis

pathways.criteria: Incidental diagnosis via MRCP or LFTs, no jaundice, no cholangitis, ALP <2x ULN
pathways.treatment: No disease-modifying therapy proven to alter natural history (UDCA showed no benefit and may be harmful at high doses; avoid high-dose UDCA >28mg/kg/day). Low-dose UDCA 13-15mg/kg/day can improve LFTs but does not delay progression. Monitor LFTs q3-6 months. MRCP q1-2 years if stable. Colonoscopy for IBD screening if not done. Annual US for gallbladder polyps (high risk of dysplasia). Surveillance for cholangiocarcinoma is controversial but consider MRCP + CA 19-9 q6-12 months in high-risk patients.

pathways.criteria: Jaundice (bilirubin >3mg/dL), ALP markedly elevated, fever/cholangitis, dominant stricture on MRCP
pathways.treatment: ERCP for therapeutic intervention: balloon dilation of dominant stricture (preferred over stenting; stents carry higher complication risk). Brushings for cytology/FISH to exclude cholangiocarcinoma. Antibiotics for cholangitis (ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + metronidazole 500mg IV q8h; or piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h). Consider ursodeoxycholic acid 13-15mg/kg/day (low-dose). Assess for dominant stricture recurrence after dilation.

pathways.criteria: Cirrhosis with portal hypertension but no decompensation, Child-Pugh A
pathways.treatment: HCC surveillance US + AFP q6m. Variceal screening EGD. Cholangiocarcinoma surveillance (MRCP + CA 19-9 q6-12m; controversial but high-risk patients). No proven medical therapy. Manage complications of cirrhosis as per guidelines. Consider transplant evaluation proactively.

pathways.criteria: Child-Pugh B/C, ascites, encephalopathy, variceal bleeding, recurrent cholangitis impacting quality of life
pathways.treatment: Transplant evaluation and listing. PSC is one of the best indications for liver transplant (excellent outcomes). Manage decompensation (diuretics, lactulose, variceal prophylaxis). Recurrent cholangitis: empiric rotating antibiotics (ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS daily). ERCP as needed for dominant strictures. Nutritional support (MCT oil, fat-soluble vitamin supplementation).

Colonoscopy with chromoendoscopy and random biopsies q1-2 years (high risk of colorectal dysplasia/cancer). PSC-IBD patients have 4x higher risk of colorectal cancer than IBD alone. Chemoprevention with mesalamine/5-ASA is recommended. PSC patients with intact colon may be considered for colectomy if high-grade dysplasia found. After transplant, continue IBD surveillance (IBD may flare post-transplant due to tacrolimus).

disease.emergencyPriority: high if acute cholangitis or dominant stricture; medium for progressive disease; low for asymptomatic
